title: The Great Stork Derby: When a Rich Man's Joke Turned Into a Baby-Making Contest
summary:
Imagine a contest where the winner is the mother who has the most babies. That may sound like a bizarre game show, but in 1920s and 1930s Canada, it was real—and it was called the Great Stork Derby.

The story begins with Charles Vance Millar, a wealthy Toronto lawyer and financier who died suddenly on Halloween in 1926. Millar was known as a prankster: he once placed money on a sidewalk and hid nearby, laughing at people's reactions. He never married or had children, and he had no living relatives. His will was full of strange provisions. He left brewery shares to Protestant ministers who supported Prohibition, and a vacation home in Jamaica to three lawyers who hated each other. But the most peculiar clause established a competition among Toronto mothers: the one who gave birth to the most children in the ten years after his death would receive a portion of his estate, valued at about 500,000 Canadian dollars (nearly 9 million today). In case of a tie, the money would be split.

The contest, dubbed the "Great Stork Derby" by the press, didn't attract much attention until the Great Depression hit. By 1930, newspapers began covering leading contenders. One was Grace Bagnato, a 37-year-old Italian immigrant who had reportedly given birth to 20 children. Another was Florence Brown, a 42-year-old "native-born Canadian of the fifth generation" who had 26 children. Brown was quoted saying she couldn't "let any Italian get away with that 'leadership stuff'" and hoped more Canadians would have large families to prevent the country from being "overrun with foreigners." The coverage revealed deep ethnic tensions in Toronto at the time.

The derby became a national sensation, sparking debates about morality, class, and immigration. Birth control was illegal in Canada until the late 1960s, and large families were controversial. The eugenics movement, which argued that poor and minority families should have fewer children, added to the controversy. Millar's intentions remain unclear, but he may have been satirizing these debates.

In 1936, the contest ended with four mothers tying for first place, each receiving a share of the fortune. The Great Stork Derby remains a bizarre chapter in Canadian history, showing how a rich man's joke could captivate a nation during hard times.
why_it_matters: This story illustrates how a single individual's eccentricity can spark widespread social debate about class, ethnicity, and morality.

keywords.explanation: A person who manages large amounts of money, often for investments.
A time when making and selling alcohol was illegal.
All the money and property a person leaves when they die.
A movement that tried to control who could have children, based on racist ideas.
Using humor to criticize or make fun of something.
A person who comes to a new country to live permanently.

questions.question: What was Charles Vance Millar's profession?
What did Millar do with his vacation home in Jamaica?
options: Left it to three lawyers who hated each other.
Left it to the winner of the derby.
questions.correct_answer: Left it to three lawyers who hated each other.

questions.question: How much was the estate worth in today's money?
Who was Florence Brown?
options: A mother who had 26 children and hoped to outdo an Italian immigrant.
A doctor who supported the contest.
questions.correct_answer: A mother who had 26 children and hoped to outdo an Italian immigrant.

questions.question: What was illegal in Canada until the late 1960s?
What is the eugenics movement?
options: A movement that argued poor and minority families should have fewer children.
A group that promoted large families.
A movement to improve children's education.
A group that opposed birth control.
questions.correct_answer: A movement that argued poor and minority families should have fewer children.

background_read: Charles Vance Millar (1853–1926) was a wealthy Toronto lawyer and financier known for his eccentric sense of humor; he never married and had no children.
At the time of the Great Depression (1930s), many Canadian families were struggling financially, and children were often seen as both a burden and a source of potential income through child labor.
Birth control was illegal in Canada until 1969, and the eugenics movement was influential, advocating for forced sterilization of people deemed 'unfit'—often poor or minority women.
Millar's will included several pranks: leaving brewery shares to Protestant ministers who supported Prohibition (which banned alcohol), and a vacation home to three lawyers who disliked each other.
The Great Stork Derby ran from 1926 to 1936, and the press coverage revealed deep ethnic and class tensions in Toronto, particularly between Anglo-Canadians and Italian immigrants.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A bizarre contest from a dead man's will – mothers racing to have the most babies.
└─ specific: 'I can't let any Italian get away with that leadership stuff' – Florence Brown
KEY EVENT: Charles Vance Millar's will creates the Great Stork Derby (1926-1936).
├─ WHO: Millar (prankster lawyer), Grace Bagnato, Florence Brown, and two other mothers
└─ AMOUNT: Prize was 500,000 CAD (9 million today)
SOCIAL CONTEXT: Great Depression, illegal birth control, eugenics movement, ethnic tensions
├─ ONE VIEW: Millar may have been satirizing debates about population and morality
└─ OTHER VIEW: The press coverage revealed deep prejudice and desperation
RESOLUTION: Four mothers tied; each got a share
LEGACY: A bizarre chapter showing how wealth and humor can impact society

perspectives.description: The winning mothers received a significant sum of money during the Depression, which could have improved their families' lives.
The contest exploited poor women and encouraged dangerous pregnancies, while the prize was trivial compared to the health risks involved.
The derby is a historical case study of media sensationalism and reveals how public debates about immigration and eugenics were framed in early 20th-century Canada.
The story raises ongoing ethical questions about inheritance, wealth, and social responsibility, and serves as a cautionary tale about the unintended consequences of eccentric wills.
